steps.title: Identify the nudge-type equivalence
steps.content: The passage explicitly states “the specific type of nudge (reciprocity, compensation, etc.) did not significantly affect user behavior.” This tells us that reciprocity and compensation nudges produce equivalent password-strength results—there's no meaningful difference between them.

steps.title: Apply the equivalence
steps.content:
Choice C is the only answer where reciprocity and compensation nudges are treated as equivalent AND where combining direct guidance with a nudge beats nudge alone.

The other choices either differentiate between nudge types, claim guidance alone is superior, or contradict the combination finding.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
